payload.text:
bir app olacak ona akademik pdf bereceğim kaynaklarında ki içeriğin doi numarasını çıkartacak listeleyecek
sonra https://github.com/Tishacy/SciDownl bu script ile hepsini indirerek sıkıştırıp bir indiriliebilir link verecek, gdrive olabilir dropbox olabilir gibi

**Developing React App Concepts**

I'm currently focused on the React app's architecture. I'm prioritizing the PDF upload and DOI extraction components. The listing of DOIs is next. I'm also considering integrating the SciDownl script for downloading papers.


**Refining Data Flow**

I've hit a roadblock with the direct file download. Given the frontend-only environment and security constraints, I've had to pivot. Now, the emphasis is on a robust PDF upload UI. I'll use Gemini to pull DOI data. Then, I'll visually simulate a downloading phase. The goal is a seamless user experience, clearly outlining the limitations.


**Adapting Download Strategies**

I've realized direct paper downloading is a server-side task, so I'm shifting to providing a list of DOIs with search links as a workaround. I can leverage Gemini effectively for DOI extraction and implement "Copy All DOIs" and Sci-Hub links. The UI structure is solidified, with the main components now defined, including a file uploader, DOI list, and Gemini integration, along with a util file for PDF conversion. I'm using a prompt specifically formatted for a JSON array for Gemini.


**Designing UI Features**

I'm now zeroing in on UI improvements to provide a better user experience. I'm focusing on a clean, academic look with clear progress indicators, using a minimalist, blue/gray color scheme. The core features will include extracted DOIs. I'll need to clearly show what the user can do next with the DOIs. I can provide the formatted DOI list, ready for use with download scripts like `SciDownl`. The app requires only standard file input permissions. Dependencies will include `lucide-react`, `framer-motion`, and `@google/genai`.
I'm self-correcting my thought process; the original plan for a direct zip download won't work in this pure frontend app. Instead, I'll provide an "Export as Text/CSV" button for the DOIs and then "Simulate" the download process, to be as functional as possible: I'll now extract DOIs using Gemini and list them with "Download" links.

entries.description: Project metadata including description and name.
Main HTML entry point with Tailwind CSS and Font support.
React application root and entry point.
Service for interacting with Gemini API to extract DOIs from PDFs.
Main application logic, state management, and layout.

payload.text:
**Analyzing the File Input**

I'm currently focused on the unresponsive PDF selection button. Examining the `App.tsx` component, I've isolated the `<input>` element responsible. My immediate aim is to understand the `onChange` event handling to pinpoint the cause of this malfunction.


**Investigating the Button Component**

I've moved on to the `Button` component, recognizing it renders a standard HTML `<button>`. This is where the click issue likely resides. It's being used within a `<label>` in `App.tsx`, creating a conflict due to the button's inherent interactivity. I need to figure out a fix, because the `Button` component in `Button.tsx` does not actually support an `as` prop.


**Refactoring the Button Component**

I'm now zeroing in on the `Button` component again. The existing implementation is rigid because it hardcodes the HTML element as a `<button>`. I'm going to update `Button.tsx` so that it supports polymorphic rendering, allowing it to render as a `span` or `div` while preserving styling. Alternatively, I can just make the styled element the label itself. I've re-examined `App.tsx` and noted that the `as="span"` prop is currently ignored. This makes fixing `Button.tsx` the best approach.

entries.description: Update Button component to support polymorphic rendering (the 'as' prop) so it can be rendered as a span inside a label without breaking accessibility or click events.
Fix the file upload trigger by ensuring the Button renders as a span when placed inside a label.

payload.text: DOI number varsa çıkartacak yoksa o makeleyi pas geçecek ama ekranda bildirim çıkacak batch command neden verdin direk indirilebilir link ve düğme ver makale isimleri yanında yazsın kullanıcı isterse indirsin tıklasın istemezse o maklaeyi indirmsin

**Prioritizing Current Requests**

I'm currently focused on prioritizing the new feature requests for the DOI extractor app. Right now, I am specifically working to implement the "skip papers without DOIs" functionality. I also plan to add a visual notification to indicate when DOIs are skipped or if there are any processing errors.


**Refining Download Functionality**

I'm now completely revamping the download strategy. Rather than a batch process, I'm opting for a direct download approach, offering a button or link for each paper. I will also incorporate article titles directly in the interface. To give users more flexibility, I'm including an individual download toggle so they can select specific articles. Finally, to handle the download, I'll provide links to `doi.org` and a search, since direct PDF access is often restricted.

entries.description: Update types to include detailed reference objects with titles and DOIs, and status tracking.
Update Gemini service to extract both title and DOI for each reference, skipping those without DOIs, and returning the count of skipped items.
Complete UI overhaul: remove batch commands, add individual reference cards with titles and direct download links, and add a notification for skipped references.

payload.text:
**Considering Frontend Implementation**

I'm focusing on incorporating `scidownl`-style functionality, but I'm constrained by being a frontend developer. My challenge is to provide direct download links/buttons, given that `scidownl` is a Python CLI tool and can't run directly in the browser. I'm prioritizing the user's need for easy, direct downloads.


**Prioritizing Direct Downloads**

I'm now zeroing in on mimicking `scidownl`'s download functionality. To give the user direct downloads, I'll include the DOI link as-is. I'll also add a "Sci-Hub Download" button that points to `sci-hub.se/{doi}`, since that's what `scidownl` frequently uses. Finally, I'll keep the Google Scholar search for manual lookup and ensure prominent titles.


**Refining Sci-Hub Link**

I've decided to prioritize a direct "Sci-Hub" link instead of simulating a download. I'll make sure the button opens a new tab with `https://sci-hub.se/` and the DOI appended. For aesthetic consistency, the color palette will be indigo/slate with high-quality icons. I'm focusing on modifying the reference card rendering and ensuring the "skipped item" notification is clear and distinct. Given the user's focus on a downloadable link, the Sci-Hub approach best fits the requirement for free access.

entries.description: Updated the UI to provide direct Sci-Hub download links (matching scidownl functionality) for each extracted DOI, added prominent article titles, and kept the skipped reference notification.

payload.text: https://sci-hub.ist/ bu adres çalışıyor bunu yerine kullan

**Updating Download Source**

I've identified the `getSciHubLink` function in `App.tsx` as the target for the URL change. Currently, my focus is solely on replacing the old sci-hub domain with the new one. This will involve a straightforward string replacement within the specified function. I anticipate minimal impact on other parts of the application.

entries.description: Updated the Sci-Hub mirror URL to use sci-hub.ist as requested by the user, ensuring direct download links remain functional.

payload.text: Implement a batch download feature that allows users to download all the extracted DOIs at once, perhaps by creating a zip file or a list of download links.

**Considering Batch Download Challenges**

I'm currently wrestling with the implications of a "batch download" feature for extracted DOIs. Downloading multiple files simultaneously in a web browser presents obstacles like security warnings and complexity. I'm exploring solutions that balance user convenience with browser limitations, weighing options to avoid disruptive pop-ups.


**Refining Batch Download Workflow**

I've shifted focus to a script generation or link opening approach for the batch download function. Given Sci-Hub's potential rate limits, direct zip creation on the client-side looks problematic due to CORS. My current plan is to improve the "Bulk Download Info" section, add an "Open All Links" button, and provide a direct "Download URLs List" option. I'm also considering a utility to generate an HTML "Research Dashboard" for local access.

entries.description: Implemented a Batch Download feature with two options: 'Open All Links' in new tabs and 'Download HTML Research Binder' which creates a portable dashboard with all extracted DOI links for offline reference.
